metadata.intendedUse: Portable clinical formulation content for Database or another repository
metadata.exclusionNote: Relationship-map structures and relationship-seeding fields are intentionally omitted by request.

dissociation.title: What Are Dissociative Disorders?, American Psychiatric Association

rumination.title: Rumination as a transdiagnostic process review, PubMed

shame.title: Shame and mental health systematic review, PubMed

perfectionism.title: Perfectionism and mental health review, PMC

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Distress drops quickly after the person escapes, postpones, suppresses, or narrows activity.
The avoided task, cue, feeling, or relationship remains threatening because it is not tested safely.
Short-term relief is followed by longer-term restriction, anxiety, shame, or functional cost.
poorFitIndicators: Withdrawal is mainly due to low energy, cognitive impairment, environmental barriers, or lack of opportunity.
The person approaches feared situations but becomes overwhelmed by dissociation, confusion, or psychosis.
The behaviour is a deliberate values-based boundary rather than a fear-maintenance cycle.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient stops attending appointments after panic symptoms in the waiting room; relief after cancelling strengthens the belief that attending is unsafe.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Plan graded appointment attendance with reduced safety behaviours and a brief post-session review of what was learned.
mechanisms.name: Avoidance
mechanisms.definition: A pattern of keeping away from internal experiences, situations, people, or tasks that trigger distress or perceived threat.
mechanisms.summary: Avoidance reduces distress in the short term but commonly maintains anxiety, trauma responses, functional impairment, and interpersonal narrowing.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Threat appraisal activates escape, safety behaviour, suppression, or disengagement, which prevents corrective learning and narrows coping options.
mechanisms.development: Often develops after repeated threat, criticism, shame, trauma, panic, social defeat, or reinforcement from short-term relief.
patientPhrases: It feels safer not to go
maintainingCycles: Threat cue -> anxiety -> avoidance -> relief -> threat belief remains untested
comparisonNotes: Differentiate from dissociation by checking whether disengagement is deliberate/behavioural or involves altered awareness.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Avoidance may explain why distress persists despite the patient working hard to stay safe.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Avoidance appears to reduce distress briefly, but it also prevents corrective experience and maintains the threat appraisal.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Target the relief-maintenance loop and build tolerable approach behaviours.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Views of self or others shift rapidly between idealised and devalued positions.
Mixed feelings feel intolerable during attachment threat, disappointment, or rupture.
Team or relationship responses become polarised around the patient’s current affective state.
poorFitIndicators: The person can hold mixed views but is making a stable, evidence-based judgement.
The main issue is fixed persecutory belief, intoxication, cognitive impairment, or cultural misunderstanding.
The shift is confined to one realistic conflict rather than a broader relational pattern.
mechanisms.caseExample: After a perceived slight, a previously trusted clinician is described as uncaring and useless, while another clinician is suddenly seen as the only safe person.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Use rupture repair to name both disappointment and continuing care without forcing premature certainty.
mechanisms.name: Splitting
mechanisms.definition: An all-or-none way of organising self, others, or situations into wholly good or wholly bad categories when mixed representations feel intolerable.
mechanisms.summary: Splitting can simplify unbearable ambivalence but may destabilise relationships, teams, and self-experience.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Conflicting positive and negative representations are not integrated, so affective state strongly shapes the current view.
mechanisms.development: May be understood psychodynamically as a primitive defence, especially under high affect, insecure attachment, or personality vulnerability.
patientPhrases: They are the only one who understands me
I was stupid to trust them
maintainingCycles: Attachment threat -> intense affect -> polarised appraisal -> rupture -> threat increases
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from ordinary ambivalence by the speed, rigidity, and interpersonal impact of the shift.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Splitting can organise otherwise overwhelming mixed feelings, but it may make alliance ruptures more likely.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Under attachment threat, splitting may make it hard to hold mixed feelings about self and others, intensifying rupture and repair cycles.
treatmentImplications: Support reflective functioning during rupture repair
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Help the patient tolerate mixed representations without escalating shame or abandonment fear.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Exposure, dependency, mistakes, or feedback trigger hiding, anger, appeasing, or self-attack.
Global self-defect language appears stronger than concern about a specific action.
Avoidance or withdrawal protects against being seen as unacceptable.
poorFitIndicators: The primary affect is guilt about a specific harm with intact self-worth.
Withdrawal is driven mainly by anhedonia, fatigue, threat, or practical barriers.
The person is responding to current stigma or mistreatment rather than internalised defectiveness alone.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient misses therapy after disclosing trauma because they feel exposed and “disgusting,” then interprets the absence as proof they are failing.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Name shame gently, separate protection from self-attack, and build tolerable disclosure or repair steps.
mechanisms.name: Shame
mechanisms.definition: A painful self-conscious affect in which the self is experienced as exposed, defective, unacceptable, or at risk of rejection.
mechanisms.summary: Shame often links trauma, attachment threat, avoidance, concealment, anger, self-criticism, and disengagement from care.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Global negative self-evaluation and anticipated social devaluation drive hiding, appeasing, attacking, or withdrawal.
mechanisms.development: May emerge from humiliation, abuse, neglect, exclusion, chronic criticism, stigma, or repeated failures interpreted as self-defect.
patientPhrases: There is something wrong with me
I cannot let anyone see that
maintainingCycles: Exposure -> shame -> hiding or attack -> isolation or rupture -> shame intensifies
comparisonNotes: Distinguish shame from guilt: shame centres on the self, guilt centres more on an action or harm.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Shame may explain avoidance, anger, withdrawal, or self-attack when the patient feels exposed or dependent.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Shame appears to organise the patient around hiding and self-protection, reducing exposure but maintaining isolation and self-criticism.
treatmentImplications: Link protective function before challenging beliefs
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Reduce concealment and self-attack while building tolerable interpersonal safety.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Affect escalates quickly and drives urgent actions before reflection is available.
The person struggles to name, modulate, or recover from emotional states.
Interpersonal, risk, or impulsive consequences feed further distress and shame.
poorFitIndicators: Mood shifts are better explained by episodic mania, delirium, intoxication, or seizure activity.
The person has intense emotion but can pause, label, and choose effective responses consistently.
The apparent dysregulation occurs only in a currently unsafe environment.
mechanisms.caseExample: After a delayed reply, a patient moves from anxiety to rage to self-harm urges within minutes, then feels ashamed and abandoned.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Use chain analysis, emotion labelling, and pause strategies before high-risk action urges dominate.
mechanisms.name: Emotional dysregulation
mechanisms.definition: Difficulty modulating the intensity, duration, expression, or behavioural consequences of emotional states.
mechanisms.summary: Emotional dysregulation is a transdiagnostic formulation mechanism that can amplify impulsivity, interpersonal rupture, avoidance, and risk.
mechanisms.coreProcess: High emotional sensitivity, intense arousal, limited regulation strategies, and rapid action urges interact under stress.
mechanisms.development: May reflect temperament, neurodevelopmental vulnerability, trauma, invalidating environments, attachment insecurity, or learned coping patterns.
patientPhrases: It goes from zero to one hundred
I do things before I can think
maintainingCycles: Trigger -> intense emotion -> urgent behaviour -> consequences -> shame or threat -> further dysregulation
comparisonNotes: Differentiate from affect intolerance by assessing whether the main issue is arousal intensity, skill deficit, or fear of emotion.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Emotional dysregulation can explain why stress rapidly becomes behavioural, interpersonal, or risk-relevant.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Emotional dysregulation appears to intensify threat cues into urgent action, creating consequences that then feed further distress.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Increase pause, labelling, and tolerable regulation options before action urges dominate.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Questions or checking repeatedly seek certainty, safety, approval, or relational confirmation.
Relief after reassurance fades quickly and the same doubt returns stronger.
Others become strained, frustrated, or pulled into repeated certainty-giving cycles.
poorFitIndicators: The person is asking for ordinary information, care coordination, or reasonable clarification.
The concern remains resolved after a clear answer or practical plan.
The main pattern is avoidance, mistrust, or protest rather than repeated uncertainty reduction.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient repeatedly asks whether the clinician is upset with them; each answer helps briefly, then doubt returns and more messages follow.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Map the reassurance loop and practise delayed reassurance, uncertainty tolerance, and internal anchors.
mechanisms.name: Reassurance seeking
mechanisms.definition: Repeatedly seeking certainty, safety, approval, or relational confirmation to reduce distress or doubt.
mechanisms.summary: Reassurance seeking can soothe briefly but may maintain uncertainty intolerance, anxiety, dependency cycles, and interpersonal strain.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Distress or doubt triggers interpersonal checking, which gives short-term relief while preventing internal tolerance of uncertainty.
mechanisms.development: Often develops where uncertainty, threat, abandonment fear, or negative self-beliefs feel intolerable without external confirmation.
maintainingCycles: Doubt -> reassurance -> relief -> doubt returns stronger -> reassurance escalates
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from ordinary support seeking by repetition, urgency, and inability to use the response.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Reassurance seeking may show how anxiety is regulated interpersonally while uncertainty tolerance remains low.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Reassurance seeking temporarily reduces doubt, but because the relief fades it can maintain dependence on external certainty.
treatmentImplications: Use response prevention or delayed reassurance where appropriate
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Shift from certainty seeking toward tolerating uncertainty and using internal anchors.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Closeness, dependency, or support is minimised just when care becomes relevant.
Self-sufficiency protects against vulnerability, intrusion, shame, or rejection.
The person distances after warmth, repair, or perceived demand.
poorFitIndicators: Distance reflects healthy boundaries, cultural preference, or a realistic safety concern.
Reduced engagement is mainly due to depression, psychosis, cognitive impairment, or access barriers.
The person wants closeness but cannot access it because of dissociation or social anxiety.
mechanisms.caseExample: After a helpful session, a patient cancels follow-up and says they do not need help, despite worsening loneliness.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Offer predictable, autonomy-respecting support and track closeness-distance shifts without shaming.
mechanisms.name: Attachment avoidance
mechanisms.definition: A relational strategy of down-regulating attachment needs, dependence, and emotional closeness to preserve autonomy or reduce vulnerability.
mechanisms.summary: Attachment avoidance may appear as self-sufficiency, emotional distance, minimisation of need, or disengagement under relational stress.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Attachment cues activate vulnerability or threat, which is managed through distance, deactivation, intellectualisation, or self-reliance.
mechanisms.development: May emerge when closeness has been unreliable, intrusive, rejecting, shaming, or unsafe.
perpetuating: Support is not tested as safe
maintainingCycles: Need -> vulnerability -> distance -> short-term safety -> unmet need persists
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from healthy independence by rigidity, distress after closeness, and cost to support or intimacy.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Attachment avoidance may explain disengagement just when support, closeness, or dependency becomes clinically relevant.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Attachment avoidance may protect against vulnerability by creating distance, but it also limits support and repair.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Make support tolerable without forcing dependency.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Events are repeatedly interpreted through broad beliefs such as defectiveness, danger, unlovability, or failure.
The belief appears across settings and shapes attention, emotion, and coping behaviour.
Disconfirming evidence is discounted, avoided, or quickly reinterpreted.
poorFitIndicators: The thought is situation-specific and changes with new evidence.
The belief reflects a current realistic threat or systemic adversity rather than a global schema alone.
The main pattern is repetitive worry or rumination without a stable self/other/world belief.
mechanisms.caseExample: A neutral email delay is read as proof the patient is disliked, confirming a wider belief of being unwanted.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Identify the recurring belief, test predictions safely, and build a more balanced alternative meaning.
mechanisms.name: Negative core beliefs
mechanisms.definition: Global, rigid, overgeneralised beliefs about the self, others, or world that shape interpretation and emotional response.
mechanisms.summary: Negative core beliefs bias attention and meaning-making, often linking early experience to current automatic thoughts, affect, and behaviour.
mechanisms.coreProcess: A schema-like belief filters events so ambiguous information is interpreted as confirming defectiveness, danger, unlovability, or failure.
mechanisms.development: May develop through repeated interpersonal learning, trauma, criticism, exclusion, loss, or chronic unmet needs.
clinicalClues: Extreme words such as always or never
maintainingCycles: Trigger -> negative meaning -> affect -> coping behaviour -> apparent confirmation of belief
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from automatic thoughts by breadth, rigidity, and recurrence across many contexts.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Negative core beliefs provide a bridge between developmental learning and current interpretations.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: A negative core belief of defectiveness appears to shape threat appraisals, self-criticism, and avoidance of disconfirming feedback.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Work at the level of meaning, not only surface thoughts.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Unwanted feelings or impulses appear attributed to another person with high certainty.
The attribution reduces self-conflict but escalates suspicion, blame, or rupture.
The content of the accusation resembles a disowned affect, wish, or fear.
poorFitIndicators: There is strong evidence that the other person is acting in the described way.
The belief is fixed, bizarre, or psychotic rather than relationally linked and affect-responsive.
The pattern is primarily shame, trauma mistrust, or negative core belief without disowned affect.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient who feels angry after a rupture insists the clinician is angry and punitive, despite no clear evidence.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Validate the affect first, then gently compare evidence and invite curiosity about the internal feeling state.
mechanisms.name: Projection
mechanisms.definition: A defence in which unacceptable feelings, impulses, or self-states are attributed to another person.
mechanisms.summary: Projection can protect against shame or conflict but may create suspicion, blame, and interpersonal escalation.
mechanisms.coreProcess: An unwanted internal state is experienced as located in the other, reducing self-conflict but distorting interpersonal appraisal.
mechanisms.development: May emerge when owning particular affects or impulses is associated with shame, danger, punishment, or loss of attachment.
precipitating: Unacceptable anger, envy, dependency, or desire
patientPhrases: They are trying to control me
maintainingCycles: Disowned affect -> attribution to other -> defensive interaction -> apparent confirmation
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from realistic mistrust by checking evidence, flexibility, and links to disowned affect.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Projection may explain why intolerable self-states are experienced as threats located in other people.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Projection may protect against owning painful feelings, but it can make relationships feel more hostile and confirming.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Build capacity to notice disowned feeling states without shame escalation.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Thinking is repetitive, past- or meaning-focused, and does not move toward effective action.
Mood worsens as the person reviews causes, failures, or consequences.
The process feels like problem-solving but remains abstract and self-critical.
poorFitIndicators: The person is doing concrete planning that leads to action.
Thinking is future-threat oriented and better described as worry.
Repetitive thought is driven by obsessional intrusions requiring a different formulation.
mechanisms.caseExample: After an argument, a patient spends hours replaying what they “should have said,” becomes more ashamed, and withdraws.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Shift from abstract replay to concrete next steps, attention shifting, or values-based action.
mechanisms.name: Rumination
mechanisms.definition: Repetitive, passive, negatively toned thinking about distress, causes, meanings, or consequences.
mechanisms.summary: Rumination may feel like problem-solving but often maintains low mood, shame, threat, and cognitive inflexibility.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Attention becomes repeatedly captured by unresolved negative meanings without moving into effective action or new learning.
mechanisms.development: May develop as an attempt to understand, prevent, or mentally undo painful experiences, especially when emotion feels unresolved.
perpetuating: Selective attention to threat or defectiveness
patientPhrases: I cannot switch my mind off
maintainingCycles: Negative event -> repetitive analysis -> mood worsens -> more negative material becomes salient
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from worry: rumination often reviews past or current distress, while worry anticipates future threat.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Rumination may maintain distress by keeping attention fixed on negative meanings without corrective action.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Rumination appears to keep the patient caught in repeated negative meaning-making, worsening mood and reducing behavioural movement.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Move from repetitive analysis to values-based action or specific problem-solving.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Thinking is repetitive, future-oriented, and organised around possible threats or uncertainty.
Planning gives a brief sense of control but increases vigilance and arousal.
The person struggles to separate solvable problems from hypothetical threats.
poorFitIndicators: The main loop is past-focused rumination rather than future threat simulation.
Concern is proportionate and leads to effective preparation.
The dominant process is intrusive obsession, trauma re-experiencing, or psychotic threat belief.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient spends the night imagining every way a meeting could go wrong and arrives exhausted but still uncertain.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Sort worries into solvable problems versus hypothetical threats, then practise uncertainty exposure.
mechanisms.name: Worry
mechanisms.definition: Repetitive future-oriented thinking about possible threats, uncertainty, or negative outcomes.
mechanisms.summary: Worry attempts to prepare for danger but may maintain anxiety by overestimating threat and under-testing coping.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Future threat simulations create an illusion of preparation while increasing vigilance, arousal, and safety behaviours.
mechanisms.development: May develop where uncertainty has felt dangerous, unpredictable, or personally costly.
maintainingCycles: Uncertainty -> worry -> temporary control -> more threat scanning -> more uncertainty
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from rumination by future orientation and perceived function of preventing harm.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Worry may function as an attempted safety strategy that keeps threat active and uncertainty intolerable.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Worry gives a brief sense of preparation, but it also sustains threat monitoring and prevents uncertainty tolerance.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Separate solvable problems from hypothetical threat loops.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: There are episodes of detachment, derealisation, depersonalisation, time loss, or altered awareness.
Disconnection appears when arousal, trauma cues, shame, or threat exceed coping capacity.
The person may look present but later reports gaps, unreality, or watching from outside themselves.
poorFitIndicators: The person is deliberately avoiding while awareness and memory remain intact.
Symptoms are better explained by seizure, intoxication, delirium, sleep disorder, or psychosis.
The main issue is emotional numbing without altered awareness or integration.
mechanisms.caseExample: During a trauma reminder, a patient feels unreal, loses track of time, and later cannot recall parts of the conversation.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Prioritise grounding, orientation, and pacing before detailed trauma processing.
mechanisms.name: Dissociation
mechanisms.definition: A disruption or disconnection in memory, identity, emotion, perception, behaviour, body experience, or sense of self.
mechanisms.summary: Dissociation can reduce overwhelming distress during threat but may later impair memory integration, presence, agency, and relational engagement.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Overwhelming arousal or threat is managed by disconnection, detachment, numbing, derealisation, depersonalisation, or memory compartmentalisation.
mechanisms.development: Frequently associated with trauma, especially repeated or overwhelming experiences, though mild dissociation can occur in everyday life.
patientPhrases: I watched it happen from outside
maintainingCycles: Threat cue -> overwhelm -> dissociation -> reduced processing -> cue remains potent
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from avoidance by altered awareness, memory, identity, or presence rather than only behavioural escape.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Dissociation may explain sudden disengagement, memory gaps, or emotional numbing when arousal exceeds coping capacity.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Dissociation appears to protect against overwhelming affect, but it may also interrupt integration and reduce present-moment agency.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Increase present-moment orientation and safety before intensive processing.

mechanisms.sourceStatus: Source metadata reviewed; owner confirmation pending
symptoms: Eating disorder symptoms in some contexts
caveats: Use as a formulation hypothesis, not as a diagnosis.
Check context, culture, developmental history, mental state, risk, and alternative explanations.
Revise the formulation when new information does not fit the hypothesised mechanism.
fitIndicators: Self-worth, safety, or acceptability depends on meeting rigid high standards.
Mistakes trigger self-criticism, overchecking, avoidance, or procrastination.
The standard remains costly even when performance is objectively adequate.
poorFitIndicators: High standards are flexible, values-based, and not associated with significant distress or impairment.
Avoidance is mainly due to fear, low mood, access barriers, or executive function difficulty.
The main belief is broad defectiveness rather than conditional worth tied to performance.
mechanisms.caseExample: A patient delays submitting work for days because anything less than flawless feels humiliating and unsafe.
mechanisms.treatmentTargetExample: Run good-enough behavioural experiments and loosen the link between worth and flawless performance.
mechanisms.name: Perfectionism
mechanisms.definition: A rigid pattern of setting excessively high standards and linking worth, safety, or acceptability to flawless performance.
mechanisms.summary: Perfectionism can drive achievement but may maintain anxiety, depression, shame, procrastination, avoidance, and burnout.
mechanisms.coreProcess: Conditional self-worth and threat appraisal attach high emotional cost to mistakes, uncertainty, or ordinary limitation.
mechanisms.development: May develop through criticism, conditional approval, achievement-based identity, insecurity, or attempts to prevent shame and loss of control.
patientPhrases: If it is not perfect, it is a failure
maintainingCycles: High standard -> threat of mistake -> overcontrol or avoidance -> temporary relief -> standard remains rigid
comparisonNotes: Distinguish from conscientiousness by rigidity, distress, and cost to functioning or relationships.
mechanisms.formulationUse: Perfectionism may organise coping around preventing shame, criticism, or loss of worth.
mechanisms.exampleSentence: Perfectionism appears to protect against shame through high control, but it increases self-criticism and avoidance when standards cannot be met.
mechanisms.treatmentLeverage: Reduce the link between worth and flawless performance.

formulationSections.label: Presenting problem
formulationSections.prompt: Brief summary of the presenting problem

formulationSections.label: Key symptoms and phenomenology
formulationSections.prompt: Observed symptoms, subjective experience, and relevant context

formulationSections.label: Predisposing factors
formulationSections.prompt: Developmental, biological, psychological, social, or cultural factors

formulationSections.label: Precipitating factors
formulationSections.prompt: Recent events, stressors, losses, conflict, or changes

formulationSections.label: Perpetuating factors
formulationSections.prompt: Maintaining cycles, avoidance, interpersonal patterns, or system factors

formulationSections.label: Protective factors
formulationSections.prompt: Strengths, supports, values, engagement, and stabilising factors

formulationSections.label: Risk formulation
formulationSections.prompt: Risk factors, protective factors, uncertainty, and monitoring needs

formulationSections.label: Treatment implications
formulationSections.prompt: Formulation-linked treatment targets and next steps

formulationSections.label: Trigger or cue
formulationSections.prompt: Situation, sensation, memory, relationship cue, or demand that starts the cycle

formulationSections.label: Meaning or appraisal
formulationSections.prompt: Beliefs, interpretations, threat meanings, shame meanings, or attachment meanings

formulationSections.label: Emotional and behavioural response
formulationSections.prompt: Affect, body response, coping behaviour, relational response, or safety strategy

formulationSections.label: Repair or stabilising step
formulationSections.prompt: Grounding, rupture repair, graded experiment, support plan, or immediate stabilising action

formulationQualityPrompts.label: Evidence supporting this mechanism
formulationQualityPrompts.prompt: What clinical observations, patient language, sequence, or behavioural pattern supports this mechanism?

formulationQualityPrompts.label: Alternative explanation
formulationQualityPrompts.prompt: What else could explain the presentation, and what would make that more likely?

formulationQualityPrompts.label: Disconfirming evidence
formulationQualityPrompts.prompt: What would weaken or overturn this formulation hypothesis?

formulationQualityPrompts.label: Intervention target
formulationQualityPrompts.prompt: What practical intervention target follows from this formulation?

avoidance__dissociation.mostUsefulDistinction: Avoidance usually preserves awareness while the person escapes or narrows activity; dissociation changes presence, memory, body experience, or awareness.
avoidance__dissociation.commonConfusion: Both can look like disengagement, shutdown, or being hard to reach in the room.
avoidance__dissociation.treatmentImplicationDifference: Avoidance often points to graded approach or behavioural experiments; dissociation first needs grounding, pacing, and orientation.
avoidance__dissociation.assessmentQuestion: When this happens, are you choosing to get away from something, or do you feel unreal, absent, detached, or missing time?

rumination__worry.mostUsefulDistinction: Rumination revisits distress, causes, and meanings; worry rehearses future threat and uncertainty.
rumination__worry.commonConfusion: Both can be described as overthinking and both can interfere with sleep and concentration.
rumination__worry.treatmentImplicationDifference: Rumination often needs concrete action or attention shifting; worry often needs problem sorting and uncertainty tolerance.
rumination__worry.assessmentQuestion: Is your mind mainly replaying what happened or trying to prevent what might happen next?

negative-core-beliefs__shame.mostUsefulDistinction: Shame is the painful exposed affect; negative core beliefs are the broader self, other, or world meanings that shame may activate.
negative-core-beliefs__shame.commonConfusion: Both can sound like global self-criticism and defectiveness.
negative-core-beliefs__shame.treatmentImplicationDifference: Shame work emphasises non-shaming contact and tolerable exposure; core belief work tests predictions and builds alternative meanings.
negative-core-beliefs__shame.assessmentQuestion: In the moment, is the strongest experience feeling exposed and unacceptable, or believing something globally true about yourself?

projection__splitting.mostUsefulDistinction: Projection locates an unwanted feeling in another person; splitting organises self or others into all-good or all-bad positions.
projection__splitting.commonConfusion: Both can produce sudden certainty, blame, and relational escalation.
projection__splitting.treatmentImplicationDifference: Projection needs gentle curiosity about disowned affect; splitting needs support for holding mixed representations during rupture.
projection__splitting.assessmentQuestion: Does the conflict centre on what the other person supposedly feels or intends, or on a rapid shift into a wholly good or bad view?
